---
title: "RMarkdown exercise"
output: html_document
date: "2026-04-27"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

## load/read the table data from URL (i.e. directly from the internet), do not manually encode the data nor store or load it from your local computer!

library(rvest)

url <- "https://dr-eberle-zentrum.github.io/Data-projects-with-R-and-GitHub/Projects/martin-raden/project-description.html"

# Read the webpage
page <- read_html(url)

# Extract tables
tables <- html_table(page, fill = TRUE)

# See how many tables were found
length(tables)

# View the first table (adjust index if needed)
data <- tables[[1]]

head(data)

## show nicely rendered table data from the loaded data, i.e. display data stored within a variable

# option a
library(knitr)

kable(data, caption = "My nicely formatted table")

# show the plot image via image PNG link, i.e. do not generate the image!

knitr::include_graphics("https://dr-eberle-zentrum.github.io/Data-projects-with-R-and-GitHub/Projects/martin-raden/Wildlife-comeback-in-Europe-1.png")


knitr::include_graphics("https://assets.ourworldindata.org/uploads/2022/05/Wildlife-comeback-in-Europe-1.png")

getwd (C:\Users\Thinkpad\Desktop\MPPSC\Elective_R2)
